meta_description: A no-frills stand-up reminder and desk break timer for office workers. Start a 30, 45, or 60 minute work timer and get nudged to take a 5-minute walking break. Backed by sedentary-health research. No login, no tracking.
timer_hit_start_again: Hit start when you sit back down.
notification_denied_banner: Notifications are blocked for this site. Enable them in your browser settings so the alarm reaches you when this tab isn't focused.
sound_storage_error: Could not save — browser storage might be full.
why_meta_description: Why short standing and walking breaks every 30 minutes matter for desk workers. Peer-reviewed research on sedentary health risks, with citations from Columbia 2023, Lancet 2016, and WHO 2020.
why_intro: Most timer apps for desk workers are over-engineered scheduling tools. This one is deliberately not. There's a real reason short walking breaks matter — here's what the research actually says, with citations, no overclaiming.
why_headline_p1: In a 2023 randomized crossover trial at Columbia, researchers tested five different sitting-break patterns over 8-hour lab sessions. Only one pattern significantly lowered post-meal blood sugar: 5 minutes of light walking every 30 minutes.
why_headline_bullet1: Lighter doses (1 min/30 min, 1 min/60 min, 5 min/60 min) reduced blood pressure but not glucose.
why_headline_bullet3: Postprandial glucose iAUC dropped roughly 12 units (p = 0.017); systolic BP dropped 4–5 mmHg across all break conditions.
why_headline_caveat: Important caveats: the study was small (n = 11), acute (single-day lab measures), and doesn't prove long-term outcomes. What it does give us is the cleanest dose-response evidence available — and the practical takeaway is that frequency matters as much as duration.
why_sitting_p1: A 2016 Lancet meta-analysis pooled data from over 1 million people across 16 studies. Sitting more than 8 hours a day was associated with a 9–59% increase in all-cause mortality — and the size of the risk depended heavily on how much someone exercised.
why_sitting_p2: The honest nuance: people in the highest activity quartile (around 60–75 minutes of moderate exercise per day) did largely offset sitting's mortality risk. That's a lot of exercise. For most people hitting the standard ~150 min/week recommendation, prolonged sitting still adds measurable risk.
why_sitting_p3: So you don't have to choose between the gym and walking breaks. You need both. What changes is that walking breaks aren't bonus credit — they're addressing something a normal exercise routine doesn't fully fix.
why_who_quote: Adults should limit the amount of time spent being sedentary. Replacing sedentary time with physical activity of any intensity (including light intensity) provides health benefits.
why_who_caveat: Important honesty point: the WHO did not set a specific time threshold (e.g., "break every 30 minutes"). The evidence wasn't strong enough. If someone tells you the WHO recommends a specific break interval, they made it up.
why_app_bullet1: Nudges you to break every 30, 45, or 60 minutes — your choice.
why_app_bullet2: Defaults to 5-minute breaks, matching the Diaz study's effective dose.
why_app_bullet3: Doesn't track streaks, score you, or guilt you.
why_app_outro: Start the timer, take a walk when it goes off, come back, start it again. That's it.
